Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain Social Identity Theory with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


Social Identity Theory (SIT), proposed by Tajfel and Turner (1979), explains how individuals drive part of their self-concept from the social groups to which they belong.

According to the theory, people first engage in social categorization, classifying themselves and others into groups, distinguishing between Ã¢ÂÂin-groupsÃ¢ÂÂ (us) and Ã¢ÂÂout-groupsÃ¢ÂÂ (them).

This is followed by social identification, where individuals adopt the norms, values, and behaviour of their in-group. 

Once group membership is established, individuals engage in social comparison, evaluating their in-group relative to out-groups in order to justify their group affiliation. 

Through this process, people seek positive distinctiveness, enhancing their self-esteem by favouring their in-group over out-groups, often leading to in-group favouritism and out-group discrimination.


Study: Tajfel and Turner

Although the boys did not initially choose their groups, the researchers' arbitrary assignment of in-groups demonstrates social categorization, as participants quickly distinguished between Ã¢ÂÂusÃ¢ÂÂ and Ã¢ÂÂthem.Ã¢ÂÂ 

Despite the lack of meaningful differences, the boys began associating primarily with members of their assigned group, illustrating social identification, whereby they perceived themselves as similar and bonded through shared group membership, leading to the formation of group norms. 

The boys then engaged in social comparison, evaluating their in-group more favourably than the out-group and perceiving the other group as inferior. 

This comparison manifested in out-group discrimination, as participants attempted to minimize the points awarded to the opposing group in the second reward system.

Simultaneously, positive distinctiveness was achieved through in-group favoritism, with participants maximizing rewards for their own group in the first reward system.

Overall, these findings support Social Identity Theory by demonstrating that even minimal and meaningless social groupings are sufficient to produce in-group favoritism and out-group discrimination, independent of real competition or conflict.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain Social Cognitive Theory with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


Social Cognitive Theory (SCT), developed by Bandura, explains how people learn behaviours by observing others. Social cognitive theory can be broken down into several different aspects but consists mostly of observational learning, that is, the ability to develop new behaviors from the viewing of a modelâs behavior and the imitation of said behaviors.

The theory highlights four key cognitive factors: attention, retention, motivation, and potential. 

Attention refers to the selective choice of what to observe as compared to ignoring other actions. 

Retention is the maintenance of the aforementioned said observations to possible use in a future situation. 

Motivation is the desire to perform a specific behavior. 
Without a need for the behavior, no imitation is likely leading to eventual loss of the attitude. 

Finally, reproduction/potential is the capability to perform the action both mentally and physically. 

To âreproduceâ an action, one must be able to do so and it must be within their limitations. 

For observational learning to occur, the model must first stand out from others to capture attention. 

Their behavior should be consistent, making it easy for the observer to understand and imitate. 

The model must also be respected, so their actions are perceived as meaningful and worth copying. 

Additionally, the model should be similar to the observer, which increases motivation and retention, as seeing someone similar succeed encourages imitation.


Study: Kimball


The findings can be explained using Social Cognitive Theory (SCT), as the children were influenced by models presented on television.

Because these models shared similar cultural backgrounds, children were more likely to pay attention to them, allowing gender-based beliefs portrayed in the media to be observed and internalized.

Boys appeared to be more affected, as male characters are more frequently represented on television, increasing the likelihood that boys attend to imitate these models. 

The children were also able to retain these observed beliefs and were motivated to reproduce them, as they possessed the physical and cognitive ability to do so. 

Overall, this demonstrates SCT, showing that children can acquire cultural norms and gender stereotypes simply through observing media models even without direct personal interaction.

Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.title: Explain one theory of the formation of stereotypes with reference to one study.
Paper 1::Sociocultural::SAQs.content:
Introduction


stereotype is a social perception of an individual based on generalized assumptions about their group membership or physical attributes.

These generalizations can be either positive or negative; for example, women may be stereotyped as skilled communicators, or conversely, as poor drivers.

Stereotypes are often acquired through socialization, such as influences from parents, peers, and broader social norms reinforced by the media, as well as through personal experience, yet they cannot be accurately applied to all members of a group.

Cognitively, stereotypes function as schemas – mental frameworks based on preconceived notions that help individuals quickly interpret and organize information about the social world.

The development and maintenance of stereotypes can be explained by Social Cognitive Theory, which proposes that humans learn behaviors, attitudes, and beliefs by overseeing and imitating others, known as models.

When a model is rewarded or punished for a behaviour, observers may be encouraged or discouraged from replicating it, a process known as vicarious reinforcement.

Imitation is more probable when the model displays consistent behaviour, is perceived as similar to the observer, and is seen as likable or admirable.

Additionally, the observer's level of self-efficacy plays a crucial role; individuals with high self-efficacy - confidence in their ability to succeed - are more willing to imitate behaviors and persist through challenges, whereas those with low self-efficacy may avoid imitation due to fear of failure.

SCT has four constructs associated with the theory:

Attention: The observer must pay attention to the model. Whether or not one pays attention to them depends on factors, such as the model’s attractiveness or authority.

Retention: Observer must be able to remember behaviour that has been observed in order to imitate it immediately or soon after.

Motivation: Observer must be able to want to imitate the behaviour, having to understand the consequences of performing that behaviour in the process.

Potential: Observer must be able to do this behaviour, physically or mentally, having enough self-efficacy to do so.


Study: Kimball


The children were influenced by models on TV, according to Social Cognitive Theory.

By attending to television models who share a similar cultural background—making them more salient and relatable—children were more likely to absorb and internalize the gender-based beliefs being portrayed.

Boys were more affected, as males tend to be on TV more often, and thus boys may pay more attention and imitate the models more.

Children were clearly able to retain these beliefs, motivating them to imitate these beliefs, which they easily have the physical and mental potential to do.

Stereotypes are perpetuated by the gender roles observed by the children on television.
